compares.label: nervous

a.label: Telling
a.why: It hands us the conclusion — nervous — and asks us to take the writer's word for it. Efficient, but we feel nothing, and we don't get to notice anything ourselves.

b.label: Showing
b.why: No one says nervous — but we arrive there ourselves, from the re-reading and the chewed pen. We got to be the noticer, the way the writer was.

compares.test: The move: find the abstract word you were about to use (nervous, sad, excited, angry) and delete it. Then write what you actually saw that made you choose that word. That evidence is the showing.

compares.label: messy room

a.label: Telling
a.why: Messy is a judgment with no picture attached. Every reader imagines a different mess — or none at all.

b.label: Showing
sentence: grew a skin by the bed, and you had to
b.why: Now the mess is specific and physical. We don't need the word messy; the cereal bowls do the whole job — and they're funnier and truer than the label was.

compares.test: The test: could a camera film it? Messy can't be filmed. Cereal bowls growing a skin can. Showing is almost always the thing a camera could catch.

compares.label: a kind teacher

a.label: Telling
a.why: We're told to admire him without being given a single reason to. The praise slides right off.

b.label: Showing
sentence: in his bottom drawer for the kids who
, and he never made it a thing.
b.why: One concrete habit — granola bars, never made a thing — and we conclude kind on our own, and believe it. Character is shown in what people do.

compares.test: The move for people: don't tell us what someone is (kind, funny, strict). Show us one thing they did, and let the reader name the quality. The reader's own conclusion sticks; yours washes off.

compares.label: fact vs. claim

a.label: Just a fact

sentence.m: has eight lines and one rhyme scheme

a.why: True — and that’s the problem. Nobody could disagree, so there’s nothing to prove. A fact isn’t a topic sentence; it’s raw material you might use as evidence later.

b.label: A claim
b.why: Debatable — a reader could push back and say the form does something else. That disagreement is exactly what gives the paragraph a job: prove it. That’s a topic sentence.

compares.test: The test: could a smart classmate reasonably disagree? If no, it’s a fact — save it for evidence. If yes, you’ve got a claim worth a whole paragraph.

compares.label: dropped vs. woven

a.label: Dropped in
a.why: The quote is dumped between two sentences with nothing holding it — a “hit-and-run” quote. The reader has to guess why it’s there and do the connecting you skipped.

b.label: Woven in
b.why: The quote is folded into your own sentence with a signal word (calls) and immediately followed by reasoning. It reads as one smooth thought — and it actually does analytical work.

compares.test: The move: never let a quotation stand as its own sentence. Introduce it (a signal phrase), then explain it (reasoning). Quote sandwiched, never quote stranded.

kernels.kernel: Everything in this poem is turning into something else — green becomes gold, leaf becomes flower becomes leaf, dawn goes down to day, Eden sinks to grief.
kernels.kernelNote: This isn’t an argument yet. It’s one noticing, lifted straight off the inventory you’d build on the Overview & Inventory page. It’s the seed. Watch the whole paragraph grow out of it — you don’t start with a thesis, you start here.
kernels.claim: Robert Frost’s “Nothing Gold Can Stay” argues that the most beautiful things in life are temporary, so we should notice them while they last.
kernels.claimNote: Ask the kernel “so what?” and it becomes a claim — a debatable one, so it needs proof. This is the topic sentence. Notice it grew from the noticing; it wasn’t decided first and stapled on.

pairs.reason: comparing new spring leaves to something precious
showing how quickly that early beauty fades
suggesting that even perfect moments end
stating the rule plainly — because golden moments fade, we should value them now

kernels.assembleNote: Each quote is evidence — but a quote alone just sits there and proves nothing. Drag each piece of reasoning to the quote it explains. That reasoning — the so what? — is the part writers skip, and it’s the part that turns a quotation into proof.
kernels.paragraph: Robert Frost’s “Nothing Gold Can Stay” argues that the most beautiful things in life are temporary, so we should notice them while they last. He starts with “Nature’s first green is gold,” comparing new spring leaves to something precious. Then “leaf subsides to leaf,” showing how quickly that early beauty fades. The allusion “Eden sank to grief” suggests that even perfect moments end. By finishing with “Nothing gold can stay,” Frost makes his point clear: because golden moments fade, we should value them right away.
kernels.note: The whole paragraph traces back to one noticing. That’s the method: notice first, ask so what? to get a claim, then prove it by pairing each piece of evidence with the reasoning that connects it. Notice the careful verbs — “suggests,” “showing” — analytical writing hedges honestly instead of overclaiming.

oimodels.text:
Nature’s first green is gold,
Her hardest hue to hold.
Her early leaf’s a flower;
But only so an hour.
Then leaf subsides to leaf.
So Eden sank to grief,
So dawn goes down to day.
Nothing gold can stay.
oimodels.source: Frost, Robert. “Nothing Gold Can Stay.” New Hampshire, Henry Holt, 1923. Public domain in the United States. Try your own overview and inventory above before revealing this one — or do the whole thing with a text you’re reading now.
oimodels.overview: A tiny poem that says the first, best moment of anything — a season, a day, a paradise — never lasts.
inventory: “Gold” and “green” both mean the new leaves, but gold sounds precious and green sounds ordinary. Why both?
Everything in it is turning into something else: green → gold, leaf → flower → leaf, dawn → day, Eden → grief.
“Her hardest hue to hold” — even nature can’t keep the good color.
It jumps from a leaf all the way to Eden. That’s a big leap for such a small poem.
Eight short lines, all rhymed. It’s over almost as fast as the gold it’s describing. (Is that on purpose?)
The last line is flat and final — “Nothing gold can stay.” No image, just the rule.
oimodels.overview2: Frost keeps every image in the middle of changing, and even the poem’s shortness enacts its point — it’s gone before you’re ready. By the last line he stops describing and just states the law: the gold moment always passes. The poem doesn’t only observe this; the compression makes you feel the hurry.
oimodels.note: Notice how Stage 2 (the second overview) grew straight out of the inventory — the “everything is turning into something else” noticing became the whole reading. That’s the method: you don’t decide what a text means and then hunt for proof. You notice, and the meaning assembles itself from what you noticed.
